title: 66.	Legendre’s formula applications (prime exponent in factorial)
description: 66.	Legendre’s formula applications (prime exponent in factorial)

examples.explanation: Compute exponent of 2 in 5! = 120. Factors: 1 (exp 0), 2 (2÷2=1, exp 1), 3 (exp 0), 4 (4÷2=2, 2÷2=1, exp 2), 5 (exp 0). Sum: 0+1+0+2+0 = 3. Verification: 120 = 2^3 × 15.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * sqrt(p))
approaches.summary: For each integer from 1 to n, compute the exponent of prime p in its prime factorization by repeatedly dividing by p, then sum these exponents. This is straightforward but inefficient for large n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Exponent in n! Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Given n and prime p, compute the exponent of p in the prime factorization of n! using the brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding how many times a prime number p divides the factorial n! (n factorial). Legendre's formula provides an efficient solution, but here we implement the brute force approach: for each integer i from 1 to n, we compute the exponent of p in i's prime factorization by repeatedly dividing i by p until it's no longer divisible, summing these exponents across all i. The intuition is that n! = 1 × 2 × ... × n, so the total exponent of p in n! is the sum of exponents of p in each factor. We iterate through each number, and for each, we repeatedly divide by p while counting how many times p divides it. This is straightforward but inefficient for large n because it requires O(n) iterations, and for each number, we perform up to O(log_p(i)) divisions, leading to O(n * log_p(n)) time, which simplifies to O(n * sqrt(p)) in worst-case analysis when considering prime checking overhead. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach connects to the broader problem by directly implementing the definition of factorial exponentiation without optimizations. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like n=0 or p=1 (p must be prime), or incorrectly summing exponents. Real code pattern: initialize total_exponent = 0; for i from 1 to n: while i % p == 0: total_exponent += 1; i //= p; return total_exponent.

examples.trace: Compute exponent of 2 in 5! = 120. Factors: 1 (exp 0), 2 (2÷2=1, exp 1), 3 (exp 0), 4 (4÷2=2, 2÷2=1, exp 2), 5 (exp 0). Sum: 0+1+0+2+0 = 3. Verification: 120 = 2^3 × 15.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Exponent of Prime in a Single Number
ladders.desc: Given an integer i and prime p, compute the exponent of p in the prime factorization of i.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: for a single number i, we determine how many times p divides i by repeatedly dividing i by p while it's divisible. The intuition is that if p is prime, the exponent of p in i is the highest power of p that divides i evenly. We start with i and a count initialized to 0. While i is divisible by p (i % p == 0), we increment the count and update i to i // p. This process extracts all factors of p from i. The time complexity is O(log_p(i)) because in each iteration, i is reduced by a factor of p, and the number of iterations is at most the exponent of p in i. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being applied to each number from 1 to n. Common mistakes include not updating i correctly (leading to infinite loops) or assuming p divides i only once. Real code pattern: def exponent_in_number(i, p): count = 0; while i % p == 0: count += 1; i //= p; return count.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Summing Exponents Across a Range
ladders.desc: Given n and prime p, sum the exponents of p for all integers from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the aggregation step: after computing the exponent of p for each individual number, we sum these values to get the total exponent in n!. The intuition is that multiplication in factorial translates to addition of exponents due to the property of exponents: if a^e1 × a^e2 = a^(e1+e2). We iterate from 1 to n, compute the exponent for each i using the sub-routine from L1, and accumulate the results. The time complexity is O(n * log_p(n)) because we perform the exponent computation for each of n numbers. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store the running sum. This is the central technique of the brute force approach, connecting to L1 by using it repeatedly and to L0 by forming the complete solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range or not resetting variables properly. Real code pattern: total = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): total += exponent_in_number(i, p); return total.

examples.trace: i=1: exponent 0, total=0; i=2: exponent 1, total=1; i=3: exponent 0, total=1. Output: 1.
i=1: exponent 0, total=0; i=2: exponent 0, total=0; i=3: exponent 1, total=1; i=4: exponent 0, total=1. Output: 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility and Division
ladders.desc: Given two integers a and b, check if b divides a, and if so, perform integer division.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two fundamental operations: checking divisibility (a % b == 0) and performing integer division (a // b). The intuition is that to find how many times a prime p divides a number i, we need to repeatedly test if p divides the current value of i and, when it does, divide i by p to reduce it. Divisibility is checked using the modulo operator, which returns the remainder; if the remainder is 0, b divides a evenly. Integer division gives the quotient without remainder, which is used to update i. This building block is essential for the exponent computation in L1. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1). It connects to L2 by being the core operation in the loop. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer division or misunderstanding modulo results. Real code pattern: if a % b == 0: a = a // b.

examples.trace: 6 % 2 == 0, so divisible; 6 // 2 = 3. Output: True, quotient=3.
7 % 3 == 1, not 0, so not divisible; no division performed. Output: False, quotient=None.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through a Range of Numbers
ladders.desc: Iterate through all integers from 1 to n inclusive.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a loop to traverse a sequence of numbers, specifically from 1 to n. The intuition is that to compute a sum or apply an operation to each number in a range, we need to access each number one by one. In programming, this is typically done with a for loop (e.g., for i in range(1, n+1):). This operation is basic but critical for the brute force approach, as it allows us to process each factor in the factorial. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration itself, and space is O(1) if no additional storage is used. It connects to L3 by providing the structure to apply divisibility checks repeatedly. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using range(n) which goes from 0 to n-1) or modifying the loop variable improperly. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n+1): # process i.

examples.trace: Loop i from 1 to 3: i=1, process; i=2, process; i=3, process; end. Output: Sequence: 1,2,3.
Loop i from 1 to 5: i=1,2,3,4,5 processed in order. Output: Sequence: 1,2,3,4,5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Factorial and Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Define factorial (n!) and prime numbers, and understand basic arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: factorial n! is the product of all positive integers from 1 to n (e.g., 5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120). A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 7). Basic arithmetic operations include addition, multiplication, division, and modulo. The intuition is that to solve the problem of finding how many times a prime divides a factorial, we need to know what a factorial is (a product) and what a prime is (a building block of numbers). This foundation connects to L4 by providing the mathematical context for looping through numbers. Common mistakes include confusing factorial with other functions or misidentifying primes. Real code pattern: n! = 1*2*...*n; prime p has only divisors 1 and p.

examples.trace: Check divisors of 5: 1 and 5 only, so it is prime. No other numbers between 2 and 4 divide 5 evenly.

approaches.name: Legendre's Formula Iterative O(log_p(n))
approaches.summary: Apply Legendre's formula: exponent = floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + floor(n/p^3) + ... until p^k > n. This reduces the problem to a simple loop over powers of p, avoiding per-integer computations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Exponent in Factorial Using Legendre's Formula
ladders.desc: Given integers n and prime p, compute the exponent of p in n! using Legendre's formula iteratively.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding how many times a prime number p divides n! (n factorial). Legendre's formula provides an efficient solution: exponent = floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + floor(n/p^3) + ... until p^k > n. The intuition is that n! includes all integers from 1 to n. Each multiple of p contributes at least one factor of p, multiples of p^2 contribute an additional factor, and so on. This avoids checking each integer individually. The algorithm initializes exponent = 0 and power = p. While power <= n, add floor(n/power) to exponent and multiply power by p. This loop runs O(log_p(n)) times. Time complexity is O(log_p(n)) as each iteration increases power exponentially. Space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This uses the iterative summation from L1 as the core computation. Common mistakes include using integer division incorrectly or stopping the loop when power > n instead of power <= n. Real code pattern: def legendre_exponent(n, p): exponent = 0; power = p; while power <= n: exponent += n // power; power *= p; return exponent.

examples.trace: Initialize exponent=0, power=2. While power <= 10: power=2, exponent += 10//2 = 5, exponent=5, power=4. power=4 <= 10, exponent += 10//4 = 2, exponent=7, power=8. power=8 <= 10, exponent += 10//8 = 1, exponent=8, power=16. power=16 > 10, stop. Output exponent=8.

Initialize exponent=0, power=5. While power <= 25: power=5, exponent += 25//5 = 5, exponent=5, power=25. power=25 <= 25, exponent += 25//25 = 1, exponent=6, power=125. power=125 > 25, stop. Output exponent=6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Summation of Floor Divisions
ladders.desc: Compute sum_{k=1}^{∞} floor(n / p^k) for given n and p until p^k > n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core summation in Legendre's formula. The intuition is to accumulate contributions from each power of p: floor(n/p) counts multiples of p, floor(n/p^2) counts multiples of p^2 (each contributing an extra factor), etc. The algorithm uses a loop: initialize total=0 and power=p. While power <= n, add floor(n/power) to total and update power *= p. This efficiently handles the infinite series by stopping when power exceeds n. Time complexity is O(log_p(n)) due to the logarithmic number of iterations. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This summation directly computes the exponent in the full problem. Connection to L2: It relies on the insight that floor division counts multiples. Common mistakes include integer overflow in power multiplication (use long integers) or incorrect loop condition. Real code pattern: def sum_floor_divisions(n, p): total=0; power=p; while power <= n: total += n // power; power *= p; return total.

examples.trace: total=0, power=2. power=2 <=10: total += 10//2=5, total=5, power=4. power=4 <=10: total += 10//4=2, total=7, power=8. power=8 <=10: total += 10//8=1, total=8, power=16. power=16 >10, stop. Output total=8.
total=0, power=5. power=5 <=25: total += 25//5=5, total=5, power=25. power=25 <=25: total += 25//25=1, total=6, power=125. power=125 >25, stop. Output total=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Multiples Using Floor Division
ladders.desc: Given n and d, compute floor(n/d) to count how many multiples of d are in range [1, n].
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight: floor(n/d) gives the count of integers in 1..n that are multiples of d. The intuition is that multiples of d are d, 2d, 3d, ..., up to k*d ≤ n, where k = floor(n/d). For example, for n=10 and d=3, multiples are 3,6,9, and floor(10/3)=3 counts them. This is efficient O(1) computation. Time complexity is O(1) for a single division. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This operation is repeated in the summation for each power of p. Connection to L3: It uses integer division. Common mistakes include using floating-point division or misunderstanding floor behavior for negative numbers (not needed here as n,d>0). Real code pattern: def count_multiples(n, d): return n // d.

examples.trace: Compute floor(10/3). 10 divided by 3 is 3.333..., floor gives 3. Multiples of 3 in 1..10: 3,6,9 → 3 numbers. Output 3.
Compute floor(25/5). 25/5=5 exactly, floor gives 5. Multiples of 5 in 1..25: 5,10,15,20,25 → 5 numbers. Output 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Floor Function
ladders.desc: Perform integer division a // b and understand floor function for non-negative integers.
ladders.explanation: This level covers integer division, which truncates toward zero, equivalent to floor for non-negative numbers. For integers a≥0 and b>0, a // b gives the quotient, discarding remainder. The intuition is how many times b fits into a completely. For example, 10 // 3 = 3 because 3*3=9 ≤10 <12. This is a fundamental operation in counting problems. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L2: This is used to compute floor(n/d). Connection to L4: It relies on basic arithmetic. Common mistakes include using / for float division or not handling b=0. Real code pattern: def integer_divide(a, b): return a // b.

examples.trace: Compute 10 // 3. 3*3=9, remainder 1, so quotient is 3. Floor(10/3)=3.333...→3. Output 3.
Compute 25 // 5. 5*5=25 exactly, quotient is 5. Floor(25/5)=5. Output 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — While Loop with Condition
ladders.desc: Implement a while loop that runs while a condition is true, updating variables each iteration.
ladders.explanation: This level involves writing a while loop, a control flow structure that repeats a block of code as long as a condition holds. The intuition is to perform repetitive tasks without knowing exact iterations upfront. For example, while power <= n: update total and power. The algorithm: initialize variables, check condition, if true execute body and update, repeat. Time complexity depends on iterations; here O(log_p(n)). Space complexity O(1). Connection to L3: This loop structure is used to sum multiple integer divisions. Connection to L5: It uses comparison operations. Common mistakes include infinite loops from not updating variables or incorrect condition. Real code pattern: total=0; power=p; while power <= n: total += n // power; power *= p.

examples.trace: Initialize power=2. Condition power<=10 true: execute, power=2, update power=2*2=4. Condition power<=10 true: execute, power=4, update power=4*2=8. Condition power<=10 true: execute, power=8, update power=8*2=16. Condition power<=10 false: stop. Output power values visited: 2,4,8,16.

Initialize power=5. Condition power<=25 true: execute, power=5, update power=5*5=25. Condition power<=25 true: execute, power=25, update power=25*5=125. Condition power<=25 false: stop. Output power values visited: 5,25,125.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Variables and Arithmetic Operations
ladders.desc: Declare integer variables, assign values, and perform basic arithmetic (multiplication, comparison).
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers working with integer variables: declaring (e.g., n=10), assigning, and using arithmetic operations like multiplication (*) and comparison (<=). The intuition is to store and manipulate numerical data. For example, to compute powers of p, start with power=p and repeatedly multiply by p. The algorithm involves simple steps: set power = p, then power = power * p. Time and space complexity are O(1) per operation. Connection to L4: These operations are used within the while loop. Common mistakes include type errors or incorrect operator precedence. Real code pattern: p=2; power=p; power=power*p;.

examples.trace: Start with power=2. Step 1: power=2*2=4. Step 2: power=4*2=8. Step 3: power=8*2=16. Output first 3 values: 2,4,8.
Start with power=5. Step 1: power=5*5=25. Step 2: power=25*5=125. Output first 2 values: 5,25.

approaches.name: Optimized Legendre with Division O(log_p(n))
approaches.summary: An optimized version of Legendre's formula where you iteratively divide n by p and sum the quotients, stopping when n becomes zero. This avoids explicit computation of powers and is more concise.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Prime Exponent in Factorial Using Optimized Legendre
ladders.desc: Given integers n and p (prime), compute the exponent of p in n! using the optimized Legendre formula.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find how many times a prime number p divides n! (n factorial). Legendre's formula states that the exponent of p in n! is the sum of floor(n/p^k) for k=1,2,3,... until p^k > n. The optimized version avoids computing powers explicitly by repeatedly dividing n by p and summing the quotients. The intuition is that each quotient floor(n/p^k) counts how many multiples of p^k are ≤ n. By summing these, we count all contributions of p to n!. The algorithm initializes count=0, then while n>0, adds floor(n/p) to count and updates n = floor(n/p). This works because floor(n/p) gives the number of multiples of p in 1..n, floor(n/p^2) gives multiples of p^2, etc., and updating n to floor(n/p) effectively computes the next term. Time complexity is O(log_p(n)) since n reduces by factor p each iteration. Space is O(1). This feeds directly into problems like counting trailing zeros in factorial or combinatorial computations. Common mistakes include not handling n=0 correctly (0! = 1, exponent is 0), using integer division incorrectly, or infinite loops if n doesn't decrease. Real code pattern: def legendre_exponent(n, p): count = 0; while n > 0: n //= p; count += n; return count.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=10, count=0. n>0, so compute n//p = 10//2 = 5, add to count: count=5, update n=5.
Step 2: n=5, n>0, compute n//p = 5//2 = 2, add to count: count=7, update n=2.
Step 3: n=2, n>0, compute n//p = 2//2 = 1, add to count: count=8, update n=1.
Step 4: n=1, n>0, compute n//p = 1//2 = 0, add to count: count=8, update n=0.
Step 5: n=0, stop. Return count=8.

Step 1: n=25, count=0. n>0, compute n//p = 25//5 = 5, add to count: count=5, update n=5.
Step 2: n=5, n>0, compute n//p = 5//5 = 1, add to count: count=6, update n=1.
Step 3: n=1, n>0, compute n//p = 1//5 = 0, add to count: count=6, update n=0.
Step 4: n=0, stop. Return count=6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Division and Summation Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the while loop that repeatedly divides n by p and accumulates the quotients.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational engine of the optimized Legendre formula. It takes n and p as inputs and returns the sum of floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + ... without explicitly computing powers. The intuition is that by updating n to floor(n/p) after each addition, we simulate moving to the next term in the series. For example, initially n is the original number; floor(n/p) counts multiples of p. Then setting n = floor(n/p) makes n represent how many groups of size p exist, so floor(n/p) now counts multiples of p^2, and so on. The algorithm: initialize count=0; while n>0: n = n // p; count += n; return count. Time complexity is O(log_p(n)) because n reduces by a factor of p each iteration (e.g., for n=100, p=5, iterations: n=100→20→4→0, 3 steps). Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the exact function to compute the exponent. Common mistakes include using floating-point division, not using integer division (// in Python), or adding the original n instead of the quotient. Real code pattern: def iterative_division_sum(n, p): total = 0; while n > 0: n //= p; total += n; return total.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=8, total=0. n>0, compute n//p = 8//2 = 4, add to total: total=4, update n=4.
Step 2: n=4, n>0, compute n//p = 4//2 = 2, add to total: total=6, update n=2.
Step 3: n=2, n>0, compute n//p = 2//2 = 1, add to total: total=7, update n=1.
Step 4: n=1, n>0, compute n//p = 1//2 = 0, add to total: total=7, update n=0.
Step 5: n=0, stop. Return total=7.

Step 1: n=12, total=0. n>0, compute n//p = 12//3 = 4, add to total: total=4, update n=4.
Step 2: n=4, n>0, compute n//p = 4//3 = 1, add to total: total=5, update n=1.
Step 3: n=1, n>0, compute n//p = 1//3 = 0, add to total: total=5, update n=0.
Step 4: n=0, stop. Return total=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Floor Division in Counting Multiples
ladders.desc: Grasp why floor(n/p) counts multiples of p up to n and how repeated division sums contributions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight behind the optimized Legendre formula. The key is that floor(n/p) gives the number of integers from 1 to n that are divisible by p. For example, for n=10, p=2, floor(10/2)=5 counts {2,4,6,8,10}. But this only counts one factor of p per number. Numbers like 4=2^2 contribute an extra factor of p. So we need to count multiples of p^2, p^3, etc. Legendre's formula sums floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + floor(n/p^3) + ... . The optimization notes that after computing floor(n/p), if we set n' = floor(n/p), then floor(n'/p) = floor(n/p^2). Thus, by repeatedly dividing n by p and summing, we compute all terms without calculating powers. Intuition: Each iteration moves to the next 'layer' of multiples. Time analysis: each division reduces n by factor p, so O(log_p(n)) steps. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the reasoning for the iterative loop. Common mistakes include misunderstanding floor division (e.g., thinking it gives exact division only) or not seeing how the sum accumulates. Real code pattern: Concept only, but example: first term = n//p, second term = (n//p)//p, etc.

examples.trace:
Compute floor(12/2)=6 → counts multiples of 2: 2,4,6,8,10,12.
Set n=6, compute floor(6/2)=3 → counts multiples of 4: 4,8,12 (each contributes extra 2).
Set n=3, compute floor(3/2)=1 → counts multiples of 8: 8 (contributes another extra 2).
Set n=1, compute floor(1/2)=0 → stop.
Sum: 6+3+1=10. So exponent of 2 in 12! is 10.

Compute floor(25/5)=5 → counts multiples of 5: 5,10,15,20,25.
Set n=5, compute floor(5/5)=1 → counts multiples of 25: 25 (contributes extra 5).
Set n=1, compute floor(1/5)=0 → stop.
Sum: 5+1=6. So exponent of 5 in 25! is 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Floor Function
ladders.desc: Perform integer division (floor division) and understand its properties.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of integer division, specifically floor division, which is crucial for the Legendre formula. Floor division of two integers a and b, denoted a // b in Python, returns the largest integer ≤ a/b. For example, 7 // 2 = 3 because 7/2 = 3.5 and floor(3.5)=3. In the context of counting multiples, floor(n/p) tells how many times p fits completely into n, which is exactly the number of multiples of p that are ≤ n. The intuition is that if you list numbers 1,2,...,n, every p-th number is a multiple of p, and floor(n/p) counts them. Properties: floor division is not the same as truncation for negative numbers, but in our problem n and p are positive. Time complexity for a single division is O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the tool to compute each term in the sum. Common mistakes include using regular division (/) which yields a float, or misunderstanding floor vs. ceiling. Real code pattern: result = n // p.

examples.trace: Compute 17 / 5 = 3.4. Floor of 3.4 is 3. So 17 // 5 = 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — While Loop with Condition and Update
ladders.desc: Write a while loop that repeats until a condition is false, updating a variable each iteration.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the programming construct of a while loop, which is essential for implementing the iterative division in the Legendre formula. A while loop repeatedly executes a block of code as long as a specified condition is true. In our case, the condition is n > 0, and we update n by dividing it by p each time. The intuition is to keep processing until n becomes zero, which happens after log_p(n) steps. The algorithm structure: initialize variables; while condition: perform operations; update variables; end while. Time complexity depends on number of iterations; here it's O(log_p(n)). Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the control flow to apply integer division multiple times. Common mistakes include infinite loops (if condition never becomes false) or off-by-one errors in updates. Real code pattern: while n > 0: # do something; n = n // p.

examples.trace:
Iteration 1: n=5, condition true, set n = 5//2 = 2.
Iteration 2: n=2, condition true, set n = 2//2 = 1.
Iteration 3: n=1, condition true, set n = 1//2 = 0.
Iteration 4: n=0, condition false, stop. Total iterations: 3.

Iteration 1: n=10, condition true, set n = 10//3 = 3.
Iteration 2: n=3, condition true, set n = 3//3 = 1.
Iteration 3: n=1, condition true, set n = 1//3 = 0.
Iteration 4: n=0, condition false, stop. Total iterations: 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Factorial and Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand what factorial (n!) means and what a prime number is.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic mathematical concepts required for the Legendre formula problem. Factorial of a non-negative integer n, denoted n!, is the product of all positive integers ≤ n. For example, 5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120. By definition, 0! = 1. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. In the problem, we are interested in how many times a prime p divides n!. The intuition is that since n! is a product, we can count the occurrences of p in its prime factorization by looking at multiples of p in the numbers 1 to n. This foundational knowledge feeds into L4 by setting up the problem context. Common mistakes include confusing factorial with other functions or not knowing prime properties. Real code pattern: Concept only, but example: def is_prime(num): if num < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(num**0.5)+1): if num % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check numbers 2 to 9: 2 (prime), 3 (prime), 4 (not prime, divisible by 2), 5 (prime), 6 (not, divisible by 2), 7 (prime), 8 (not, divisible by 2), 9 (not, divisible by 3). So primes: 2,3,5,7.
